quiz_questions.question_text: The LSRL minimizes:

options.text: The sum of residuals
The sum of squared residuals
The correlation
The intercept

quiz_questions.explanation: Least squares means minimizing Σ(residual)² = Σ(y − ŷ)².

quiz_questions.question_text: The slope b represents:

options.text: The average change in ŷ for each one-unit increase in x
The correlation
The y-intercept
The sum of squares

quiz_questions.explanation: Slope = the rate of change in the predicted response per unit change in x.

quiz_questions.question_text: The LSRL always passes through:

options.text: The point (x̄, ȳ)
The origin

quiz_questions.explanation: The line passes through the means of x and y.

quiz_questions.question_text: ŷ = 10 + 3x. When x = 5, ŷ = ?
The y-intercept (a) is the predicted y when:

options.text: x = x̄

quiz_questions.explanation: The intercept is where the line crosses the y-axis (x = 0).

quiz_questions.question_text: b = r · (sᵧ/sₓ). If r is positive then b is:

options.text: Negative
Positive
Undefined
Zero

quiz_questions.explanation: The sign of the slope always matches the sign of r because standard deviations are positive.

quiz_questions.question_text: If b = 2.5, increasing x by 1 predicts ŷ increases by:
quiz_questions.explanation: The slope gives the predicted change in y per unit increase in x.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sum of all residuals from the LSRL equals:

options.text: A positive number
A negative number

quiz_questions.explanation: By construction, the LSRL has residuals that sum to zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: If r = 0.8, then r² = ?
quiz_questions.explanation: r² = 0.8² = 0.64, meaning 64% of the variation in y is explained by the regression.

quiz_questions.question_text: Switching x and y in regression:

options.text: Doesn't affect the slope
Changes the value of r
Gives the same line
Gives a different regression line

quiz_questions.explanation: The regression of y on x differs from the regression of x on y, although r remains the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the least squares regression line?

options.text: Any line drawn through a scatterplot
The unique straight line that minimizes the sum of the squared vertical distances from each data point to the line
A line that passes through every data point
The line connecting the first and last data points

quiz_questions.explanation: The LSRL is the line that makes the total squared prediction error as small as possible.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the slope of a regression line tell you?

options.text: The predicted average change in the response variable for each one-unit increase in the explanatory variable
The predicted value of y when x is zero
The total variation in y
The strength of the correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope is always interpreted as the average change in y per one-unit increase in x.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the y-intercept of a regression line represent?

options.text: The slope of the line
The mean of the response variable
The point at which the line crosses the x-axis
The predicted value of y when the explanatory variable x equals zero

quiz_questions.explanation: The y-intercept a is the predicted value of the response when x = 0, though it may not always have a meaningful real-world interpretation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is extrapolation in regression?

options.text: Using the regression line to predict y within the range of observed x values
Removing outliers before fitting the line
Calculating the correlation coefficient
Using the regression line to predict y for x values outside the range of observed data, which is unreliable

quiz_questions.explanation: Extrapolation extends the model beyond the data range, where the linear pattern may no longer hold.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does r² mean in the context of regression?

options.text: The average residual
The residual standard deviation
The square of the number of data points
The proportion of total variation in y that is explained by the linear relationship with x

quiz_questions.explanation: r² (coefficient of determination) indicates how well the regression line accounts for the variability in the response.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'residual' mean in regression?

options.text: The difference between the observed y value and the predicted y value (y − ŷ)
The value of x at the mean
The predicted value of y
The square of the correlation

quiz_questions.explanation: A residual measures how far the actual observation is from the value predicted by the regression line.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean when the intercept has no real-world meaning?

options.text: x = 0 falls outside the range of the data, so the intercept is just a mathematical anchor and the model is still useful for prediction within the data range
The slope is also meaningless
The intercept should be set to zero
The model is completely invalid and should not be used

quiz_questions.explanation: When x = 0 is not realistic (e.g., a person with 0 years of education), the intercept is just a mathematical starting value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'interpolation' mean in regression?

options.text: Fitting a curved line instead of a straight line
Predicting y for x values within the range of observed data, which is generally reliable
Calculating the sum of residuals
Predicting y for x values outside the data range

quiz_questions.explanation: Interpolation stays within the observed x-range, where the model is most trustworthy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for the slope of the LSRL?

options.text: b = ȳ − a × x̄
b = r × (sₓ / sᵧ)
b = r × (sᵧ / sₓ)
b = Σ(x − x̄)

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope equals the correlation times the ratio of the standard deviation of y to the standard deviation of x.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'predicted value' (ŷ)?

options.text: The mean of all y values
The value of y estimated by the regression line for a given x
The residual
The actual observed y value in the dataset

quiz_questions.explanation: ŷ is the output of the regression equation for a specific x value, representing the expected average y.

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate agent fits a regression: ŷ = 50,000 + 120x, where x is square footage and ŷ is predicted sale price in dollars. A 2,000 sq ft house recently sold for $310,000. What is the residual?

options.text: $20,000 (the house sold for more than predicted)

quiz_questions.explanation: ŷ = 50,000 + 120(2,000) = $290,000. Residual = 310,000 − 290,000 = $20,000, meaning the house sold for $20,000 more than predicted.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nutritionist models the relationship between daily calories consumed (x) and weight gain over a month (y, in pounds) for 35 adults: ŷ = −8 + 0.005x. What does the slope mean in context?

options.text: Each additional calorie predicts a 0.005-pound increase in monthly weight gain, on average
The person will lose 8 pounds if they eat nothing
Each additional pound gained requires eating 0.005 more calories
0.005 is the correlation coefficient

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope is interpreted in context: for each additional calorie consumed daily, the model predicts 0.005 additional pounds of weight gain per month.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher has the regression ŷ = 40 + 6x, where x = hours studied and ŷ = predicted test score. She wants to predict the score for a student who studied 12 hours, but students in the dataset studied between 1 and 8 hours. What concern applies?

options.text: The prediction of 112 is reliable because the equation is valid
The model should be recalculated with x = 12 included
This is extrapolation — predicting outside the observed range — and the linear trend may not hold at 12 hours
The slope changes outside the observed range

quiz_questions.explanation: Predicting at x = 12 when data only goes up to x = 8 is extrapolation; the relationship may level off or change beyond 8 hours.

quiz_questions.question_text: An environmental scientist fits ŷ = 15.2 − 0.03x, where x = parts per million of a pollutant and ŷ = fish population (thousands) in a lake. r² = 0.74. Which interpretation is best?

options.text: 74% of the variation in fish population is explained by the linear relationship with pollutant level
The regression explains all the variation in fish population
The pollutant reduces the fish population by exactly 74%
74% of lakes have this pollutant

quiz_questions.explanation: r² = 0.74 means that 74% of the variability in fish population across the studied lakes can be accounted for by the linear association with pollutant concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car manufacturer models fuel efficiency: ŷ = 45 − 0.005x, where x = vehicle weight (lbs) and ŷ = miles per gallon. For a 3,000-lb car, ŷ = 45 − 0.005(3000) = 30 mpg. The actual fuel efficiency is 28 mpg. What is the residual and what does it mean?

options.text: Residual = 2; the car is more efficient than predicted
Residual = 30; the prediction equals the residual
Residual = −2; the car gets 2 mpg less than the model predicted
Residual = 0; the prediction is exact

quiz_questions.explanation: Residual = y − ŷ = 28 − 30 = −2. The negative residual means the actual mpg was 2 less than the model predicted.

quiz_questions.question_text: A college admissions office uses high school GPA (x) to predict first-year college GPA (y): ŷ = 0.5 + 0.7x, r² = 0.49. A counselor claims 'GPA in high school causes success in college.' Is this claim justified by the regression alone?

options.text: Yes, because r² is close to 0.5
No, because r² must be above 0.75 to establish causation
Yes, because the slope is positive
No, regression shows association but cannot prove causation; other factors like study habits may be involved

quiz_questions.explanation: Regression establishes association, not causation. Lurking variables like motivation and study skills could explain the relationship.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer records inches of rainfall (x) and crop yield in bushels per acre (y) over 20 seasons. The LSRL is ŷ = 30 + 4.5x. In a season with 10 inches of rain, the yield was 80 bushels/acre. Is this above or below what the model predicted?

options.text: Below — the model predicted 85 bushels/acre, so the residual is −5
Cannot be determined from this information
Exactly on the line — residual is 0
Above — the model predicted 75 bushels/acre, so the residual is +5

quiz_questions.explanation: ŷ = 30 + 4.5(10) = 75. Residual = 80 − 75 = +5. The actual yield was 5 bushels/acre above the prediction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marketing analyst models monthly website visits (y, in thousands) against advertising spending (x, in thousands of dollars): ŷ = 12 + 8x. She warns against using the model to predict visits for $50,000 in spending when the data only covered $1,000 to $10,000. Why?

options.text: The slope would change to a negative value at higher spending levels
Spending $50,000 is extrapolation far beyond the data range, and the linear relationship may not hold at such high spending levels
The model would predict negative visits
The intercept is unreliable

quiz_questions.explanation: At $50,000, the model predicts ŷ = 12 + 8(50) = 412 thousand visits, but diminishing returns likely apply at high spending, making this extrapolated prediction unreliable.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health researcher fits ŷ = 130 − 0.8x, where x = minutes of daily exercise and ŷ = systolic blood pressure (mmHg). The data ranged from x = 0 to x = 90 minutes. For x = 0, ŷ = 130. What does the intercept mean in this context?

options.text: The intercept has no real-world meaning because no one exercises zero minutes
130 is the maximum possible blood pressure
People who don't exercise have an average predicted blood pressure of 130 mmHg
Blood pressure increases by 130 mmHg per minute of exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Since x = 0 is within the data range, the intercept has a meaningful interpretation: the predicted systolic blood pressure for someone who does not exercise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school district collects data on class size (x, number of students) and average standardized test score (y). The LSRL is ŷ = 95 − 0.4x with r² = 0.36. An administrator considers reducing class size from 30 to 20 students. According to the model, what is the predicted change in average test score?

options.text: A decrease of 4 points
An increase of 0.4 points
No change, because the model only works for individual students
An increase of 4 points, since reducing class size by 10 at a rate of 0.4 points per student gives 0.4 × 10 = 4

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope is −0.4, so each additional student is associated with a 0.4-point decrease. Reducing by 10 students predicts a 0.4 × 10 = 4-point increase.
